Details
Error: Maximum update depth exceeded. This can happen when a component repeatedly calls setState inside componentWillUpdate or componentDidUpdate. React limits the number of nested updates to prevent infinite loops.

            "text": "  // If it's not production environment, it would be a better idea to inform that the App is disabled."

          {
            "lineNumber": 23,
            "text": "  if (props.isAppDisabled) {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 24,
            "text": "    if (!IS_PRODUCTION) {"

          {

            "text": "            here"
          },

            "lineNumber": 31,
            "text": "          \u003c/Link\u003e"
          },

            "lineNumber": 32,
            "text": "        \u003c/div\u003e"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 33,
            "text": "      );"

          {
            "lineNumber": 34,
            "text": "    }"

          {
            "lineNumber": 35
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 36,
            "text": "    // Disabled App should give 404 any ways in production."

            "text": "  }"

            "lineNumber": 51,
            "text": "async (ctx: AugmentedNextPageContext) =\u003e {"

          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 77,
            "text": "  if (res) {"

          {
            "lineNumber": 78,
            "text": "    // Running on the server, the response object is available."
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 79,
            "text": "    //"

          {
            "lineNumber": 80,
            "text": "    // Next.js will pass an err on the server if a page's &#96;getInitialProps&#96;"

          {

            "text": "    // threw or returned a Promise that rejected"
          },

            "text": "    // Overrides http status code if present in errorInitialProps"

            "text": "    res.statusCode = errorInitialProps.statusCode;"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 85
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 86,
            "text": "    log.debug(&#96;server side logged this: ${err?.toString() ?? JSON.stringify(err)}&#96;);"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 87,
            "text": "    log.info(\"return props, \", errorInitialProps);"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 88
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 89,
            "text": "    return errorInitialProps;"
          },

            "lineNumber": 90,
            "text": "  } else {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 91,
            "text": "    // Running on the client (browser)."
          },

            "lineNumber": 92,
            "text": "    //"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 93,
            "text": "    // Next.js will provide an err if:"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 94,
            "text": "    //"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 95,
            "text": "    //  - a page's &#96;getInitialProps&#96; threw or returned a Promise that rejected"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 96,
            "text": "    //  - an exception was thrown somewhere in the React lifecycle (render,"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 97,
            "text": "    //    componentDidMount, etc) that was caught by Next.js's React Error"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 98,
            "text": "    //    Boundary. Read more about what types of exceptions are caught by Error"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 99,
            "text": "    //    Boundaries: https://reactjs.org/docs/error-boundaries.html"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 100,
            "text": "    if (err) {"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 101,
            "text": "      log.info(\"client side logged this\", err);"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 102,
            "text": "      return errorInitialProps;"

            "lineNumber": 103,
            "text": "    }"
          },
          {

            "text": "  }"
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 105
          },
          {
            "lineNumber": 106,
            "text": "  // If this point is reached, getInitialProps was called without any"
          },

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Now I can clearly see the problem. There are **two** places calling `initState` with `props` as a dependency:

1. **Line 53-55 in `CalendarInner`**:
```javascript
useEffect(() => {
    initialState(props);
}, [props, initialState]);
```

2. **Line 191-195 in `Calendar`**:
```javascript
useEffect(() => {
    if (storeRef.current) {
      storeRef.current.getState().initState(props);
    }
}, [props]);
```

The issue is that `props` is an object that gets a new reference on every render. When `initState` is called, it calls `set(...)` on the Zustand store, which triggers a re-render of `CalendarInner` (because it subscribes to the store via `useCalendarStore`). 

Since `{...props}` spreads into a new object reference each render, the `useEffect` dependency on `props` fires repeatedly. The `initState` function updates the Zustand store, which causes `CalendarInner` to re-render with fresh props, creating a new object reference and triggering the effect again—an infinite loop.

Both `Calendar` and `CalendarInner` have this same issue with `props` as a dependency, and both call `initState`, so I need to either use individual prop values as dependencies instead or prevent the props object from being recreated on each render. when `props` changes. Since `Calendar` is called from `BookingCalendarView` which creates new objects like `events` (a useMemo) and functions like `onEventClick` (an inline arrow function), the `props` will have a new identity on every render of `BookingCalendarView`.

But the key infinite loop is specifically from `CalendarInner`:
1. `CalendarInner` reads from the store (lines 29-41)
2. `CalendarInner` has a useEffect that calls `initialState(props)` when `props` changes  
3. `initialState(props)` calls `set({...state, ...})` which changes the store
4. Since `CalendarInner` reads from the store, any store change re-renders it
5. When re-rendered, `props` is a new object (because `Calendar` does `<CalendarInner {...props} />`)
6. New `props` reference → useEffect fires → `set()` → store changes → re-render → cycle repeats

So I need to break this cycle by either comparing the actual prop values instead of their references, or by extracting the meaningful dependencies as primitives before passing them to the effect dependency array. Let me check what the `CalendarComponentProps` type looks like to see which approach makes more sense.</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Now I have a clear picture of the bug. The issue is in `Calendar.tsx` in two places:

1. **`CalendarInner` (line 53-55)**: 
```js
useEffect(() => {
    initialState(props);
}, [props, initialState]);
```

2. **`Calendar` (line 191-195)**:
```js
useEffect(() => {
    if (storeRef.current) {
      storeRef.current.getState().initState(props);
    }
}, [props]);
```

Both use `props` as a dependency. Since `props` is an object, it gets a new reference on each render. 

This creates a problematic cycle: `initState` updates the store, triggering re-renders of `CalendarInner` which subscribes to it, giving it new `props`, which fires the useEffect again. The real culprit is `CalendarInner`'s useEffect since it's directly subscribed to the store, plus `BookingCalendarView` passes inline arrow functions like `onEventClick` that create fresh references every render. The cleanest solution is removing the duplicate `initState` call in `CalendarInner`.

Looking at the actual flow, `Calendar` spreads its props into `CalendarInner`, both call `initState` in their useEffects, and `CalendarInner` subscribes to the Zustand store—so when `initState` updates the store, it triggers a re-render of `CalendarInner`, the spread creates a new props object, the useEffect fires again, and we're stuck in a loop. The fix is to remove `CalendarInner`'s useEffect entirely since `Calendar` is already handling the prop-to-store synchronization. The issue is that `props` gets a new reference whenever `BookingCalendarView` re-renders due to inline function definitions, which triggers `Calendar`'s useEffect repeatedly. However, since `Calendar` itself doesn't subscribe to the store, those `initState` calls won't cause `Calendar` to re-render, so there's no feedback loop there—the infinite loop must be coming from somewhere else in the chain.

Actually, I see it now: `CalendarInner` subscribes to the store and re-renders on updates, and its useEffect depends on `props` and `initialState`. When `initialState` calls `set()` to update the store, that triggers `CalendarInner` to re-render, which gives `props` a new reference (since `Calendar` passes them through), which fires the useEffect again, creating the cycle. The solution is to remove the useEffect from `CalendarInner` entirely since `Calendar` is already handling the prop-to-store synchronization.

But wait—`Calendar`'s useEffect has the same vulnerability. When `BookingCalendarView` re-renders and passes new props to `Calendar`, it triggers `Calendar`'s useEffect, which calls `initState` and updates the store, causing `CalendarInner` to re-render. However, `Calendar`'s useEffect will still fire unnecessarily on every parent re-render because `props` always gets a new identity (like when `onEventClick` is an inline function), leading to redundant store updates even if it doesn't create an infinite loop. The real fix is to remove the duplicate useEffect dependency and stabilize the props reference. infinite loop itself, but is wasteful. _**Agent (claude-opus-4-6)**_

I've identified the root cause. The infinite loop is in `apps/web/modules/calendars/weeklyview/components/Calendar.tsx`. Here's the chain:

1. `CalendarInner` subscribes to the Zustand store via `useCalendarStore` (lines 27-41)
2. `CalendarInner` has a `useEffect` that calls `initState(props)` with `[props, initialState]` as dependencies (lines 53-55)
3. `initState` calls `set(...)` which updates the store
4. Store update triggers `CalendarInner` to re-render (since it subscribes to the store)
5. On re-render, `props` gets a new object reference (because `Calendar` does `<CalendarInner {...props} />`)
6. New `props` reference causes the `useEffect` to fire again -> back to step 2 -> infinite loop

The `Calendar` wrapper component (lines 183-202) already handles syncing props to the store in its own `useEffect`, so the duplicate `useEffect` in `CalendarInner` is both redundant and the cause of the infinite loop.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6)**_

The fix is clean. Reload the page in your browser and the infinite loop should be gone.

**What was happening:** `CalendarInner` both subscribed to the Zustand store (via `useCalendarStore`) and called `initState(props)` in a `useEffect`. Since `initState` calls `set(...)` on the store, it triggered a re-render of `CalendarInner`. On each re-render, `props` got a new object reference (from `<CalendarInner {...props} />`), which triggered the `useEffect` again — creating an infinite loop.

**The fix:** Removed the redundant `useEffect` and `initialState` variable from `CalendarInner`. The parent `Calendar` component already handles syncing props to the store via its own `useEffect` at line 185, and it doesn't subscribe to the store, so no feedback loop occurs there.

Found it. Looking at the git history, the latest commit (`48ce1136a2`) changed `BookingCalendarView.tsx` — specifically, it changed a mount-only `useEffect` from `[]` to `[currentWeekStart, onWeekStartChange]`. **That** is what broke things.

Here's the chain:
1. `BookingCalendarView`'s `useEffect` fires and calls `onWeekStartChange(currentWeekStart)`
2. The parent updates state → passes a new `currentWeekStart` dayjs object back down
3. `BookingCalendarView` re-renders → the `useEffect` fires again (new dayjs reference = new dependency) → infinite loop
